title: Focal Atrial Tachycardia
authors: William H. Sauer, Paul C. Zei

items.title: Mechanisms and Clinical Presentation of Focal Atrial Tachycardia (AT)
items.content: Focal AT arises from abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, or small reentry circuits in diseased atrial tissue. It can originate from various atrial regions including pulmonary veins and the coronary sinus. Clinically, it presents with 1:1 AV conduction or AV block patterns (e.g., Wenckebach or fixed). P waves are often discrete with an isoelectric segment, distinguishing AT from other SVTs.
keyPoints: Mechanisms include abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, small reentry circuits.
~10% of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVTs) in catheter ablation cases are focal AT.
P waves often discrete with an isoelectric segment, unlike atrial flutter or macroreentrant AT.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.title: Distinguishing Focal AT from Sinus Tachycardia and Other SVTs
items.content: Focal AT can be differentiated by its P-wave morphology, which varies based on the origin within the atria. Unlike sinus tachycardia, focal AT may have a shorter P-R interval compared to R-P interval due to rapid AV nodal conduction. A warm-up or cool-down phase is indicative of enhanced automaticity in AT.
keyPoints: P-wave morphology varies with focus location; e.g., left atrial origin shows monophasic positive P wave in V1.
Focal AT may have a shorter P-R interval than R-P interval when 1:1 conduction is present.
Warm-up or cool-down phases suggest enhanced automaticity, distinguishing AT from AV nodal-dependent SVTs.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.title: Management and Clinical Implications of Focal AT
items.content: Asymptomatic nonsustained focal AT identified on ECG monitoring does not require treatment. However, symptomatic or frequent atrial ectopy may necessitate therapy similar to that for focal AT. Incessant AT can lead to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Focal AT is often associated with conditions causing atrial fibrosis and can be a precursor to more significant arrhythmias.
keyPoints: No treatment recommended for asymptomatic nonsustained focal AT on ECG monitoring.
Symptomatic or frequent atrial ectopy may require therapy similar to focal AT.
Incessant AT can cause tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.question: Which underlying mechanisms are associated with focal atrial tachycardia (AT)?
options: Abnormal automaticity, triggered automaticity, and a small reentry circuit in diseased atrial tissue
AV nodal reentry and accessory pathway conduction
items.explanation: Focal AT is associated with abnormal automaticity, triggered automaticity, or a small reentry circuit in diseased atrial tissue.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.question: What percentage of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardias (PSVTs) is accounted for by focal AT in patients referred for catheter ablation?
items.explanation: Focal AT accounts for ~10% of PSVTs in patients referred for catheter ablation.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.question: What is the recommended treatment approach for asymptomatic nonsustained focal atrial tachycardia identified on ECG monitoring?
items.explanation: Treatment is not recommended for asymptomatic nonsustained AT identified on ECG monitoring.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.question: Which characteristic feature distinguishes focal atrial tachycardia from AV nodal-dependent SVTs?
options: Dependent on AV nodal conduction for termination
P waves with an intervening isoelectric segment
1:1 AV conduction or AV block in a Wenckebach pattern
items.explanation: Focal AT typically presents with 1:1 AV conduction or with AV block in a Wenckebach or fixed pattern, distinguishing it from AV nodal-dependent SVTs.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.statement: Frequent atrial ectopy and nonsustained AT are often precursors to more significant arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: Frequent atrial ectopy and nonsustained AT can be precursors to more significant arrhythmias like atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.statement: Symptoms from focal atrial tachycardia are always severe.
items.explanation: Symptoms from AT are highly variable and similar to other supraventricular tachycardias.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.assertion: AT can occur in the absence of structural heart disease.
items.reason: Areas of fibrosis can act as a nidus for abnormal automaticity from injured but partially living cells or microreentry in zones of slow conduction within and on the border of fibrotic areas.
items.explanation: AT can occur without structural heart disease, while the reason relates to conditions with atrial fibrosis.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia

items.assertion: A warm-up phase is common in AV nodal-dependent SVTs.
items.reason: A warm-up phase when the atrial activation rate increases after initiation favors AT rather than AV nodal-dependent SVT.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a warm-up phase is more indicative of AT, not AV nodal-dependent SVTs.
items.reference: Focal Atrial Tachycardia · Focal Atrial Tachycardia
